question: Is the NuScale RPV design pressure (Table 5.3-1) adequate for the operating conditions listed in Table 1.2-1?

nuscale_ch05_0014.title: Reactor Vessel
nuscale_ch05_0014.summary: The Reactor Vessel section describes the NuScale Power Module (NPM), which integrates the reactor core, steam generators, and pressurizer within a single reactor pressure vessel (RPV) surrounded by a containment vessel. The RPV serves as a critical barrier to fission product release, supports various reactor components, and is constructed from materials selected to ensure long-term integrity in compliance with ASME Boiler and Pressure Vessel Code standards.

nuscale_ch05_0005.title: System Components
nuscale_ch05_0005.summary: This section describes key components of the NuScale reactor system. It details the Reactor Pressure Vessel (RPV), which houses the reactor core and supports major systems, and outlines the main Reactor Coolant System (RCS) piping and its connections. The section also briefly introduces the pressurizer (PZR), which is integrated into the RPV.

nuscale_ch01_0015.title: General Plant Description
nuscale_ch01_0015.summary: This section provides a summary of the NuScale plant’s overall design, including its main systems such as safety features, power conversion, fuel handling, cooling, waste management, and auxiliary systems. It explains that each COL applicant must reference the NuScale FSAR and provide site-specific details, with certain conceptual design information and security details clearly identified and separated from the certified design scope.

nuscale_ch05_0017.title: Reactor Vessel Integrity
nuscale_ch05_0017.summary: This section outlines the procedures and regulatory compliance measures to ensure the integrity of the NuScale reactor vessel over its 60-year design life. It details the development of operating procedures to maintain pressure-temperature limits, evaluates the vessel’s resistance to pressurized thermal shock and upper-shelf energy degradation, and confirms that predicted material properties remain within regulatory safety criteria throughout the vessel’s operational lifespan.

nuscale_ch01_0072.title: Combined License Information Items
nuscale_ch01_0072.summary: This section explains the interface requirements between the NuScale Power Plant’s certified design and the site-specific design provided in the Combined License (COL) application. It distinguishes between conceptual design information (CDI) for non-certified plant portions and COL information items, which are site-specific responsibilities of the COL applicant. The section also notes that COL applicants must address all COL information items and provide a list of any departures from the certified design.

nuscale_ch05_0023.title: Reactor Coolant System High-Point Vents
nuscale_ch05_0023.summary: This section outlines the testing and inspection requirements for the DHRS (Decay Heat Removal System) components in the NuScale reactor, ensuring compliance with ASME codes for Class 2 components. It specifies that DHRS actuation valves are exercised during cold shutdown conditions, with additional testing for valve position and actuator power, and includes a one-time in-situ test of the DHRS function during initial reactor startup.

nuscale_ch05_0006.title: System Evaluation
nuscale_ch05_0006.summary: This section describes the evaluation of the NuScale Power Plant’s system performance, focusing on optimizing operational parameters such as coolant temperatures, flow rates, and pressure levels to maximize efficiency and safety. Calculations using thermal hydraulic software and hand verification establish best-estimate, maximum, and minimum flow conditions, accounting for uncertainties and ensuring low pressure losses to support the plant’s natural circulation design.

nuscale_ch01_0052.title: Comparison with Other Facilities
nuscale_ch01_0052.summary: This section compares the major design features and parameters of the NuScale Power Plant with those of a typical pressurized water reactor (PWR). It highlights differences in size, output, system components, and safety systems, showing that NuScale uses a smaller, simpler, and more integrated design compared to conventional PWRs. The comparison is summarized in tables for easy reference.

nuscale_ch05_0024.title: Pressurizer
nuscale_ch05_0024.summary: The pressurizer in the NuScale reactor is integrated into the upper region of the reactor vessel and functions to regulate reactor coolant system (RCS) pressure by maintaining a saturated steam-water interface. It uses heaters and spray flow to control pressure and is designed to handle operating transients without triggering reactor trips or safety system actuations, while providing continuous monitoring and control through instrumentation.

nuscale_ch01_0679.title: DSRS 15.1.5, Rev 0: Steam System Piping Failures Inside and Outside of ContainmentII.1 AssumptionsInitial Power Level and Plant Operating Mode
nuscale_ch01_0679.summary: This section summarizes NuScale’s conformance with regulatory criteria for steam system piping failures both inside and outside containment, as outlined in DSRS 15.1.5. It confirms that the plant meets all specific criteria and assumptions—including initial power level, plant operating mode, loss of offsite power, postulated steam line break effects, and worst-case single active component failure—without exceptions. The evaluation also notes that critical heat flux is used for core damage assessment, and all safety-related classifications and radiological criteria are met.

nuscale_ch01_0112.title: Figure 1.7-3a: Piping and Instrumentation Diagram Legends
nuscale_ch01_0112.summary: This section provides the legend for piping and instrumentation diagrams (P&IDs) used in the NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report, detailing the symbols and notations for various valves, vents, drains, fire safety equipment, and specialty items. It specifies standard representations, such as 0.75" normally closed valves for all vents and drains unless otherwise noted, and includes notes on symbol usage and system abbreviations.

nuscale_ch05_0009.title: Overpressure Protection
nuscale_ch05_0009.summary: This section outlines compliance with regulatory requirements for overpressure protection of the reactor coolant boundary, referencing specific ASME Boiler and Pressure Vessel Code (BPVC) and Operations and Maintenance (OM) code cases as mandated by 10 CFR 50.55a and related Regulatory Guides. It details how code cases are selected, applied, and documented for design, fabrication, construction, preservice, and inservice inspections and testing, ensuring all conditions set by the NRC are met. Table 5.2-1 consolidates the specific code cases used.

nuscale_ch01_0676.title: DSRS 15.1.5, Rev 0: Steam System Piping Failures Inside and Outside of ContainmentII.2 Specific CriteriaEvaluation of Core Damage Potential
nuscale_ch01_0676.summary: This section summarizes NuScale’s conformance with regulatory criteria for steam system piping failures inside and outside containment, specifically regarding the evaluation of core damage potential. It states that NuScale uses critical heat flux as a more accurate descriptor of plant phenomena than departure from nucleate boiling and confirms compliance with all relevant criteria and assumptions outlined in DSRS 15.1.5.

nuscale_ch05_0016.title: Pressure-Temperature Limits, Pressurized Thermal Shock, and Charpy Upper-Shelf Energy Data and Analyses
nuscale_ch05_0016.summary: This section outlines the methodology and regulatory compliance for establishing pressure-temperature limits for the reactor coolant pressure boundary (RCPB) over the plant’s 60-year life. It describes how these limits are calculated using ASME and NRC guidelines, accounting for vessel embrittlement, and provides the resulting curves and data to ensure protection against non-ductile failure during various operational conditions.

nuscale_ch01_0113.title: Figure 1.7-3b: Piping and In strumentation Diagram Legends
nuscale_ch01_0113.summary: This section provides the legend for piping and instrumentation diagrams (P&IDs) used in the NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report, detailing standard symbols and abbreviations for valve positions, actuator types, and valve status during normal operation. It explains the meaning of terms such as NO (normally open), NC (normally closed), FO (fail open), and describes various actuator mechanisms and their operational states. This legend ensures consistent interpretation of P&ID drawings throughout the documentation.

nuscale_ch01_0706.title: DSRS 15.2.1-15.2.5, Rev 0: Loss of External Load; Turbine Trip; Loss of Condenser Vacuum; Closure of Main Steam Isolation Valve; and Steam Pressure Regulator Failure (Closed)II.2.B Fuel Cladding Integrity
nuscale_ch01_0706.summary: The section confirms that the NuScale design meets the regulatory criteria outlined in DSRS 15.2.1-15.2.5 for events such as loss of external load, turbine trip, loss of condenser vacuum, closure of the main steam isolation valve, and steam pressure regulator failure (closed), specifically regarding fuel cladding integrity. The design does not include a steam pressure regulator, and for all reviewed criteria—including incidents of moderate frequency, instrument setpoints, single failures, and performance of nonsafety-related systems—NuScale is shown to conform with no exceptions noted.

nuscale_ch01_0019.title: Operating Characteristics
nuscale_ch01_0019.summary: The NuScale Power Module (NPM) operates using natural circulation for reactor coolant flow, eliminating the need for pumps, and features a small, modular, integral pressurized water reactor design with passive safety systems that do not rely on electrical power. The reactor and containment are partially immersed in water, providing a passive heat sink for decay heat removal, and the system allows for flexible power maneuvers using control rods and soluble boron. Key components, including the reactor core, steam generators, and pressurizer, are integrated within the reactor pressure vessel, reducing external piping and enhancing safety.

nuscale_ch01_0709.title: DSRS 15.2.1-15.2.5, Rev 0: Loss of External Load; Turbine Trip; Loss of Condenser Vacuum; Closure of Main Steam Isolation Valve; and Steam Pressure Regulator Failure (Closed)II.2.E Most Limiting Plant System Single Failure
nuscale_ch01_0709.summary: The section confirms that the NuScale design meets regulatory criteria for events such as loss of external load, turbine trip, loss of condenser vacuum, main steam isolation valve closure, and steam pressure regulator failure (closed), even though the design does not include a steam pressure regulator. For each scenario, the analysis finds no additional concerns or non-conformances, and the most limiting plant system single failure is addressed with no exceptions noted. The design is shown to conform with the relevant sections of the Standard Review Plan and Design Specific Review Standard.

nuscale_ch01_0177.title: DSRS 6.6, Rev 0: Inservice Inspection and Testing of Class 2 and 3 ComponentsII.6 System Pressure Tests
nuscale_ch01_0177.summary: This section summarizes NuScale’s conformance with regulatory criteria for inservice inspection and testing of Class 2 and 3 components, specifically regarding system pressure tests. It confirms that NuScale meets all relevant requirements outlined in DSRS 6.6, Rev 0, and notes that certain SRP sections related to ice condensers and suppression pools are not applicable to the NuScale design.

edges.description: Section A describes the design and function of the NuScale reactor pressure vessel (RPV), while Section B outlines operational procedures and pressure conditions that the RPV must withstand, thus connecting the vessel's design parameters to its required performance under specified operating conditions.

Section A provides specific design details of the NuScale RPV, while Section B outlines the general operating conditions and criteria that the RPV must satisfy, connecting them through the requirement that the RPV design pressure must be adequate for the operating conditions described in Section B.

Section A describes the physical design and function of the NuScale reactor pressure vessel (RPV), while Section B discusses the development of operating procedures to ensure that the RPV's pressure-temperature limits (as specified in design tables like Table 5.3-1) are not exceeded during operation, directly connecting the vessel's design values to their application in operational safety.

Section A describes the reactor pressure vessel (RPV) as a component of the certified design, while Section B identifies which structures, systems, and components (including the RPV) are part of the certified design per Table 1.2-1, thus connecting the RPV's design features to the regulatory scope.
Section B provides a general overview of the plant's operating characteristics, which are further detailed in Section A's description of specific system components and operational procedures.

Section B states that operating procedures are developed to ensure pressure-temperature limits (such as the RPV design pressure from Table 5.3-1) are not exceeded during the operating conditions (as listed in Table 1.2-1), thereby ensuring adequacy.

Section B describes the operational process of venting nitrogen from the pressurizer (PZR) through the RPV high point degasification line to match containment pressure, which is a scenario that Section A's inspection and classification requirements for DHRS components (including valves and piping involved in venting) are intended to address, ensuring these components function properly under such operating conditions.

Section A details inspection and classification requirements for specific reactor coolant system components, while Section B provides a general overview of the plant's systems, including those referenced in Section A, thus Section A specifies details for the general descriptions found in Section B.

Section A discusses compliance with ASME and NRC overpressure protection requirements for the reactor coolant boundary, while Section B details specific ASME code compliance for reactor vessel attachments; both sections reference adherence to ASME BPVC standards, connecting them through regulatory code compliance for RPV integrity.

Section A outlines regulatory criteria and conformance for pressure-related safety analysis, while Section B provides the legend for interpreting valve positions on piping and instrumentation diagrams, which is necessary for understanding the system configurations referenced in Section A's analysis.

Section A discusses regulatory criteria and conformance for accident scenarios affecting fuel cladding integrity, while Section B describes system component operations and pressure management relevant to those scenarios, connecting the adequacy of RPV design pressure to the operating conditions.

Section A lists the operating conditions that the RPV must withstand, while Section B describes the RPV as the pressure-retaining component designed to handle those conditions.

Section A lists the operating characteristics and conditions under which the NuScale RPV must function, while Section B describes system component behaviors and pressure management actions that must accommodate those operating conditions, connecting the adequacy of RPV design pressure to the actual operational scenarios.

Section B provides the design details of the NuScale reactor pressure vessel (RPV), including its pressure-retaining function, which must be evaluated against the operating conditions referenced in Section A to determine if the design pressure is adequate for postulated events.

Section B provides the general plant description and operating characteristics, which include the operating conditions referenced in the analysis question, while Section A discusses the NuScale design's conformance with regulatory criteria for system failures that could challenge the RPV design pressure.

Section A discusses the NuScale reactor's conformance with regulatory criteria for accident scenarios affecting fuel cladding integrity, while Section B addresses inservice inspection and pressure testing requirements for system components, both of which are connected by the need to ensure that the RPV design pressure is sufficient to withstand operational and accident conditions as required by regulations.

Section A describes the NuScale module's operating characteristics, which establish the normal and transient conditions that the RPV must withstand, while Section B outlines regulatory requirements and accident scenarios (such as turbine trip and loss of condenser vacuum) that the RPV design pressure must be adequate to handle.

Section A describes the NuScale reactor's operating characteristics relevant to pressure and design, while Section B clarifies that certain regulatory criteria for pressure systems (ice condenser and suppression pool) are not applicable to NuScale, connecting them by indicating which regulatory requirements are relevant for evaluating the adequacy of the RPV design pressure.
